name: llups_minimal_example
summary: Compact 1S2P 18650 power module providing regulated 5V and 3.3V from USB-C charging input, suitable for a battery-backed embedded project. Topology-level plan only; exact part numbers and schematic are out of scope for this layer.

user_spec.raw_text: I want a small power supply board that runs off two 18650 cells in parallel, charges over USB-C, and gives me a regulated 5V and 3.3V output. It should keep working while charging and protect the cells from over-discharge. Roughly business-card sized.

clarifications.question: What's the maximum continuous current you need on the 5V rail?
Do you have a preferred charge controller IC family, or should I pick one?
clarifications.answer: I've used the BQ24072 before and like it. Use that family if it fits.

clarifications.question: Any compliance constraints (FCC, CE)?
clarifications.answer: No, this is for personal/lab use only.

functional: Charge a 1S2P 18650 pack from USB-C input.
Provide a regulated 5V output rail at up to 1A continuous (1.5A peak).
Provide a regulated 3.3V output rail at up to 500mA.
Continue powering outputs from USB while charging (power-path).
Disconnect the load before the cells reach a damaging discharge voltage.
electrical: USB-C input, default 5V (no PD negotiation required).
Battery operating range: 2.8V (cutoff) to 4.2V (full charge) per cell.
Quiescent current under load-disconnect should be sub-100uA.
mechanical: Board outline approximately business-card sized (about 90mm x 58mm).
Two 18650 cells mounted on-board in 1S2P configuration.
interfaces: Header pins or pads exposing VBAT_RAW, 5V, 3.3V, and GND.

must_use_parts.name: BQ24072
must_use_parts.reason: User has prior experience and prefers this charger family.

explicitly_excluded: USB Power Delivery negotiation (not required, adds complexity).

blocks.description: USB-C receptacle providing the 5V charge source plus inline ESD/transient protection.
Two protected or unprotected 18650 cells wired in parallel, single-cell voltage.
Charges the cell pack from the USB input while passing power through to the system load.
blocks.rationale: User-pinned BQ24072 family; topology block role abstracts the pinning so a substitute can be chosen if BQ24072 is unavailable.
candidate_part_classes: Li-ion charger IC with integrated power-path

blocks.description: Disconnects the load when cell voltage falls below a safe threshold.
candidate_part_classes: Single-cell Li-ion protection IC with low cutoff threshold (~2.8V)

blocks.description: Higher-threshold supervisor that gates downstream regulators before the hard protection trips, to keep the system in a defined state.
candidate_part_classes: Voltage supervisor IC with threshold around 3.3V and load-switch output

blocks.description: Boosts the 2.8-4.2V battery rail to a regulated 5V at 1A continuous, 1.5A peak.
Linear regulator providing a quiet 3.3V at up to 500mA, fed from VBAT or 5V rail.
Through-hole header or castellated pads exposing VBAT_RAW, 5V, 3.3V, GND.

open_questions: Should the 3.3V LDO be fed from the 5V boost (better PSRR, lower efficiency) or directly from VBAT (better efficiency, marginal headroom near cutoff)?
Is reverse-current protection on the USB-C VBUS pin needed if the host can be back-fed by the charger?
What is the desired charge rate (1A vs 2A)? Affects ISET resistor and thermal sizing of the charger block.

research_notes.note: BQ24072 family integrates dynamic power-path management so the system load can be powered from USB while the battery charges; this matches the 'continue powering outputs while charging' requirement.

next_layer_hints.preferred_topology_variant: linear charger + boost + LDO (no buck-boost), single-cell pack, hardware-only protection
